tweets.text:
US consumer sentiment points to further job market weakness:

The gap between consumers saying jobs are "plentiful" versus "hard to find" fell to just 2.4 points in June, the lowest since the 2020 pandemic.

Just 24.9% of consumers now say jobs are "plentiful," down from ~55.0% in 2022, while 22.5% say jobs are "hard to find," up from ~10.0% over the same period, and the highest since January 2021.

Historically, this measure has been one of the most reliable leading indicators of rising unemployment, and it now suggests the US unemployment rate could rise to as high as 6.0%, from the current 4.2%.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate, which measures the working-age population of those either employed or looking for a job, fell to 61.5% in June, the lowest since June 1976, excluding the pandemic period.

This comes as the labor force dropped -720,000 last month, to 169.36 million, the lowest since December 2024.

The job market is much weaker than headlines suggest.
tweets.full_text:

The gap between consumers saying jobs are "plentiful" versus "hard to find" fell to just 2.4 points in June, the lowest since the 2020 pandemic.

Just 24.9% of consumers now say jobs are "plentiful," down from ~55.0% in 2022, while 22.5% say jobs are "hard to find," up from ~10.0% over the same period, and the highest since January 2021.

Historically, this measure has been one of the most reliable leading indicators of rising unemployment, and it now suggests the US unemployment rate could rise to as high as 6.0%, from the current 4.2%.

Meanwhile, the labor force participation rate, which measures the working-age population of those either employed or looking for a job, fell to 61.5% in June, the lowest since June 1976, excluding the pandemic period.

This comes as the labor force dropped -720,000 last month, to 169.36 million, the lowest since December 2024.

The job market is much weaker than headlines suggest.

tweets.text:
Investor sentiment in the energy market is rapidly shifting:

Energy funds posted -$3.2 billion in outflows in the week ending July 1st, the largest weekly withdrawal since July 2024.

This is also the 2nd-biggest weekly outflow in at least 10 years.

Energy funds also saw -$1.5 billion in outflows the prior week, the largest since April 2025.

As a result, the 4-week average of outflows is up to -$1.8 billion, the biggest on record.

This marks a sharp reversal from a record +$2.5 billion in the 4-week average of inflows seen just 2 months ago.

Investors are aggressively rotating out of the energy sector.

tweets.text:
We now believe the S&P 500 is setting up for 8,000+. Here's why:

Chip stocks have quietly become the new leaders of this bull market.

While many of the Magnificent 7 stocks have declined 20%+ from their recent highs, semiconductor names have taken over.

In fact, 8 of the 10 best-performing S&P 500 stocks this year are in the chip industry.

As a result, the S&P 500 now sits just ~1% below a record high despite weakness the big tech stocks which have led the market since 2022.

This marks the first time since 2022 that the market traded highly with leadership outside of the Magnificent 7, while the Magnificent 7 traded in the opposite direction.

In our view, this rotation is constructive as the Magnificent 7 prepares to "sub back in."

As large-cap tech begins to regain leadership while semiconductors continue to outperform or even cool-off, the S&P 500 is poised for 8,000+.

Asset owners will continue to win.

tweets.text:
Humanoid robots should create new jobs, not replace them.

Historically, major technological shifts, from the tractor to the internet, faced the same fear of job displacement, but that fear was wrong. US labor productivity has compounded at ~2.1% annually since the commercial tractor's debut. Real disposable income has grown at a ~3.2% annualized rate over the postwar period.

ARK's research suggests both will accelerate meaningfully over the next five to ten years as generalizable robots scale.

But here is the catch: a humanoid robot is 200,000 times more complex than a robotaxi. Reaching human-level task proficiency will require ~$50 billion in compute investment and years of real-world data collection.

Tesla's Optimus could approach that threshold around 2028. China is pushing hard to get there first.

tweets.text:
@RealJimChanos @perry_lin1 Nice try but 1) $1M/year maintenance capex assumes replacing entire facility before lease expires. No way.
2) If triple-net lease, then Anthropic pays the maintenance capex outside of facility-level which is maybe $100-200k/MW/year. $1-2M would include IT equip refresh.

thread_tweets.text:

thread_tweets.full_text:
@RealJimChanos @perry_lin1 Nice try but 1) $1M/year maintenance capex assumes replacing entire facility before lease expires. No way.
2) If triple-net lease, then Anthropic pays the maintenance capex outside of facility-level which is maybe $100-200k/MW/year. $1-2M would include IT equip refresh.

thread_tweets.text:
1) Maintenance capex is cash spent on upkeep in addition to depreciation.
2) Data center shells genuinely do have long useful lives: Commercial real estate useful life under GAAP is 39 years. Hyperscaler-grade purpose-built data centers (concrete, steel, custom power infrastructure) realistically last 30–40+ years. 
3) Even if you depreciate over 25 years straight-line ($360K/MW/yr D&A on $9M/MW capex), WULF's building is fully depreciated before the lease expires. That means the renewal options at years 20–30 are nearly pure cash flow on a depreciated asset. His model conveniently ignores this optionality entirely (which is even more relevant for $IREN, incidentally).

I remember a 2024 dinner hosted by a "well-connected" investment bank whose name rhymes closely enough with "rentier."

They assured me Tier 1 hyperscalers would never sign infrastructure deals with Bitcoin miners, while underwriting most leveraged digital asset crapcos.

A few months later, they hired my analyst.

Needless to say, our relationship has changed.

tweets.text:
I did not always believe in the four year cycle for Bitcoin.

I used to think it was silly, and that the market had to be more complicated than that.

But then the market proved me wrong. So I admitted I was wrong, learned from my mistakes, and became a better investor.

This cycle, a lot of new Bitcoiners faded the four year cycle and relentlessly mocked anyone calling for the bear market. For many of them, instead of recognizing they were wrong, they continue to laugh at anyone willing to discuss the simplicity of the four year cycle.

They are likely doomed to repeat the same mistakes in the future, since their ego gets in the way of them being able to admit that they were wrong.

tweets.text:
After some thought, I still don't agree with the red card call on Balogun, as the other player ran into him from behind which arguably caused him to stumble in an awkward way. I'm sure many will disagree with that as the still image looks pretty bad. But if you look at how the play developed, I think it provides some missing context. I think Balogun deserved a yellow card, but not a red.

With that said, two wrongs do not make a right. I'm not thrilled with the US president getting involved in the matter. The truth is that if this were any other team, I would not be happy about the unfair treatment. Yes, Ronaldo was able to play in the world cup after a similar ruling, but in this case the ruling occurred after the US president called FIFA, which optically is bad.

I wish that red cards did not cause the player to miss future games against a team that was not even involved with the red card, but that is how the rules are written. And if other teams have to abide by those rules, then so too should the USA.

I will be pulling for the USA tonight regardless of whether Balogun plays or not, but I think it would be good for the integrity of the game for presidents to not contact FIFA to inquire about referee decisions.

tweets.text:
Manufacturing has expanded for 6 straight months. The June ISM PMI hit 53.3.

Normally, the S&P 500 has done well after that, with an 83% win rate and an average gain of 5.5% over the next 6 months.

This time, the Shiller PE is 41.60, only 5.9% below the 1999 peak. It has only been this high in 1999 and 2000. The last similar setup was June 1999.                       

Read full analysis: https://t.co/Twf6dVLdj4
tweets.full_text:
Manufacturing has expanded for 6 straight months. The June ISM PMI hit 53.3.

Normally, the S&P 500 has done well after that, with an 83% win rate and an average gain of 5.5% over the next 6 months.

This time, the Shiller PE is 41.60, only 5.9% below the 1999 peak. It has only been this high in 1999 and 2000. The last similar setup was June 1999.                       

Read full analysis: https://t.co/Twf6dVLdj4

I am fascinated by all the controversy over this Balogun move... This is standard World Cup stuff! I mean... have you all forgotten the 2002 World Cup? Or where the last TWO World Cups were hosted!? Everyone's acting like Trump is corrupting the Vatican here (although even that is probably a bad example!). 

Also... can we pump the breaks on Balogun? Like the whole thing is meaningful, but despite the celebration move, he is not actually LeBron. It's not like the US went out and got some Cameroonian ex-pat to complete their roster. 'Na mean? 

(Not bitter at all...)

tweets.text:
I lived in Quebec for eight years ... I love it. Everything about Quebec, but especially l'hiver. 

One thing I learned about Quebec is that it is quite wonderfully parochial. It just does not care much about what the rest of the world is doing. Its always going to be dancing to its own tune, and that's great. I salute that. In today's world, ignorance really is bliss. 

The problem is that we no longer live in 1995, and I am not sure that sovereignists in Quebec get that. Back in the heyday of America's magnanimous hegemony and globalization, it made sense for Quebec to become independent and simply melt into the world (as say, Slovenia did). Today, with the US openly invoking territorial expansion in North America,  the moment has passed. 

It was always adorable to me that my Quebecois friends would use the example of "what happened to the francophones of Louisiana" as the reason to secede from Canada. When it is precisely the reason to stay in Canada. 

What's more likely? Quebec to lose its identity within Canada (we kind of have 300 years of evidence that it will not!) or within a North America whose borders are changing? 

Let me be blunt to all the sovereignists out there: there is zero chance that this America lets you have your francophone/socialist paradise alongside St. Lawrence in a multipolar world. Zero. Not gonna happen. Non. 

The future for Quebec is to redefine Canadian identity. To usurp it. It's Canadian to be francophone. Here, let me help: 

Quebec: Canada depuis 1534

https://t.co/SuYo11iu8O
